text: The surgeon examines the pelvis and uses a trans-abdominal proline suture to suspend the uterus, facilitating the opening of the recto-vaginal septum.
The surgeon makes an initial incision on the peritoneum at the level of the sacral promontory, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs a careful and precise dissection to open the lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, revealing the anatomical structures within the pelvic region.
The surgeon carefully manipulates tissues to avoid injuring the mesorectal vessels during the robotic ventral mesh rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon identifies and carefully preserves the hypogastric nerves during the procedure, ensuring these structures remain intact within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a three-dimensional robotic camera to enhance visualization during the procedure, providing a clearer view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a combination of monopolar and bipolar diathermy to achieve a clear and precise view of the surgical field, effectively controlling bleeding during the procedure.
An incision is made over the peritoneum lining the pouch of Douglas, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the recto-vaginal septum and uses traction and counter traction to develop the plane between the rectum and the vagina, carefully separating these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses endo-wrist instruments to access the recto-vaginal septum, facilitating a safe dissection in this area.
The surgeon encounters varying degrees of scarring in the surgical field, visible through the three-dimensional robotic camera, as they continue the dissection in the recto-vaginal area.
The surgeon continues dissection in the recto-vaginal septum, further developing the plane between the rectum and vagina using traction and counter traction.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the recto-vaginal septum, advancing towards the level of the perineal body, with visible separation of tissue planes.
The surgeon exposes the white fibers of the perineal body during the dissection in the recto-vaginal septum, highlighting the anatomical structure.
The surgeon reaches the stage where the rectum is visibly separated from the vaginal wall, highlighting the anatomical boundary between these structures.
The surgeon performs a rectal examination to confirm that the dissection has reached the correct level, located at the top of the anal sphincter complex.
The surgeon prepares a mesh for suturing, which is visually positioned near the dissected recto-vaginal septum.
A 5x15 centimeter strip of polypropylene mesh with a bowel-friendly coating on one side is prepared for use in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays an iPalm mesh, which is prepared for use in the procedure.
The surgeon uses an Ethibon II-O suture to secure the prepared iPalm mesh to the rectum, ensuring it is properly attached at the correct level of dissection.
Robotic endo-wrist instruments are used to facilitate suturing deep in the pelvis, enhancing precision and ease in the confined surgical space.
The surgeon places careful suture bites into the rectal wall, ensuring adequate tissue engagement without penetrating the full thickness of the wall.
The surgeon continues to secure the iPalm mesh to the rectum using an Ethibon II-O suture, ensuring a stable fixation with the robotic endo-wrist instruments.
The surgeon uses sutures to attach the iPalm mesh to the top of the sacrum at the sacral end, securing it in place.
The surgeon uses a sliding knot technique to adjust the tension in the mesh, ensuring it is securely positioned within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon places additional sutures to securely anchor the mesh to the rectal wall, ensuring stable fixation without full thickness penetration.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect using a running suture technique with a 2-0 V-Loc suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon ensures that the entire mesh is positioned extraperitoneally at the end of the procedure, confirming its placement outside the peritoneal cavity.
